1:
The mightiest of the prophets enters without prelude, and the witnesses first supply the setting his abruptness leaves unexplained. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, opening the whole book, frame Elijah against Ahab's apostasy: not satisfied with the sin of Jeroboam, the king had made the worship of Baal the national religion, built it a temple in his capital, and let Jezebel hunt the prophets of Jehovah; and against this the Lord raised up a prophet who "attested his name" אליהו, "whose God is Jehovah," working with a power of God no other prophet in the northern kingdom matched. Charles Ellicott does the philology, reading with the LXX and Josephus "Elijah the Tishbite of Tishbe in Gilead," a phrase that distinguishes his home from a Tishbe in Naphtali, and he paints Gilead as the rocky region east of Jordan, wild and open to the desert, a fit haunt for so recluse a dweller in the wilderness.

The Reformed John Gill adds the older topography — Gilead divided among Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh — and traces the name to a town Toshab or Thisbe. But for every school the weight falls on the oath Elijah swears, "As the Lord God of Israel liveth, before whom I stand." Ellicott hears in it the whole man, "the servant of God standing to be sent whither He wills," and notes that this cannot be Elijah's first appearance, since James 5:17 makes the drought the answer to a prophet's prayer, implying earlier struggle and warning against the Baal-worship of the age.

The Puritan Matthew Henry turns the sudden entrance into the lesson itself: Elijah "drops, so to speak, out of the clouds," like Melchizedek without recorded descent, so that some Jews fancied him an angel — until the apostle assured us he was "a man subject to like passions as we are." Henry reads those passions as a strong and eager temper suited to the hour: "Rough spirits are called to rough services. The reformation needed such a man as Luther to break the ice." His obscure birth is no prejudice, "for we need not enquire whence men are, but what they are"; and the shutting of heaven is as much a matter of prayer as of prophecy, the power by which God's witnesses "have power to shut heaven, that it rain not" (Revelation 11:6).

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke catalogues the apocryphal fables about Elijah's parentage and reports the strange conjecture that he had no earthly parentage at all, an angel joined for a time to a body — yet grants it "irreconcilable" with James's word that he was a man of like passions. Clarke also preserves Jarchi's rabbinic legend that Ahab taunted Elijah with Moses' warning that apostasy would shut the heavens (Deuteronomy 11:16) provoking the very oath. Beneath the legends the schools are one: a lone man, of no account by birth, is made the instrument of God's controversy with idolatry, and the key of the clouds is placed in the hand of prayer.
2:
A single clause — "and the word of the Lord came unto him" — carries the whole movement of the story forward, and with only two witnesses at hand the reading turns on what that word does. It is the recurring hinge of the chapter, sounding again when the brook fails (v.8), and the Reformed John Gill, following the Targum, presses that this is "the word of prophecy." From it he draws a small but pointed inference: it proves that when Elijah said in v.1 that neither dew nor rain should fall "but according to my word," he meant the word of the Lord spoken by him, not a will of his own. The prophet commands the heavens only as the mouth of the God before whom he stands.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch ask why that word, once spoken, drives Elijah immediately into hiding, and their answer, borrowed from Sebastian Schmidt, is arresting: the concealment was ordered "not so much that he might be safe from the wrath and pursuit of Ahab and Jezebel, as to preclude all earnest entreaties to remove the punishment." Having announced that the rain would return only at his word, Elijah must be put beyond reach, lest the desperate land wear him down with pleading and he be moved to revoke the very judgment God meant to accomplish. The command to "turn eastward" (קֵדְמָה) moves him from Samaria, where he had confronted the king, toward the Jordan and its ravines.

The two readings meet in a single truth: the word that shuts heaven also governs the prophet who spoke it. Gill's insistence that the word is the Lord's, and Keil and Delitzsch's picture of a herald hidden lest he undo God's sentence, guard the same point — Elijah is not master of the drought but servant of the word, sent to vanish as promptly as he was sent to speak. What in v.3 becomes an itinerary is here still only a summons; the same voice that made him a herald now makes him a fugitive, and the obedience the chapter will celebrate begins with his silent withdrawal at a command he did not choose. That God should hide his own witness in the day of judgment is itself a mercy withheld: the prophet who might have interceded is removed, that the chastisement may run its appointed course until the word again brings rain.
3:
The summons of the previous verse now becomes an itinerary, and the witnesses divide chiefly over a point of geography — which bank of the Jordan the brook Cherith lay upon. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch weigh the Hebrew עַל־פְּנֵי, which can mean both "to the east of" (as in Genesis 25:18) and simply "in the face of" or "before" (as in Genesis 16:12), and conclude that the brook was in any case one emptying into the Jordan, though its side cannot be fixed with certainty. Charles Ellicott reads it as "the torrent facing the Jordan," one of the ravines running down into the valley, and inclines to place it on the east, "probably on the east from the prophet's own land of Gilead," where the recluse would be at home.

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke lays out both opinions — some set Cherith on the western side near Samaria, others on the eastern, since the prophet is told to go eastward — and turns at once to the necessity of the flight: after so bold a declaration to a wicked and idolatrous king, Elijah must instantly hide himself, "as, on the first drought, Ahab would undoubtedly seek his life." But Clarke will not leave the passage on the map. He hears in the whole arrangement a proof of the power of God and the vanity of idols: "As God's prophet prayed, so there was rain or drought; and all the gods of Israel could not reverse it. Was not this sufficient to have converted all Israel?"

The Reformed John Gill adds only that the prophet was to turn from the place where he was, "being in danger from Ahab and Jezebel, provoked by his reproofs, threatenings, and prophecies." The debate over the brook's bank is genuine but friendly, and the witnesses do not press it far; where they agree is weightier. The God who sends the drought also appoints the hiding-place, naming the very stream from which his servant shall drink and commanding, before Elijah ever arrives, the ravens that shall feed him (v.4). Providence does not merely permit the prophet's flight but charts it, so that obedience is a matter of following, step by step, a way already prepared.
4:
The provision at Cherith is double — drink from the brook, food by the ravens — and the witnesses fasten on the second as the chapter's first great miracle. The Wesleyan Adam Clarke keeps it brief here, promising fuller treatment at the close of the chapter (v.24), and states the plain sense: "Thou shalt not lack the necessaries of life; thou shalt be supplied by an especial providence." The Reformed John Gill is plainer still: the water of the brook "was to be his drink," the whole point being that the God who dried the land keeps a table for his servant in it.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott carries the real argument, and he carries it against the rationalizers. The rendering "ravens," he insists, is "that of almost all the ancient versions and of Josephus, and there can be little doubt" of it; the "singularly prosaic interpretations" that substitute "Arabs," "merchants," or "inhabitants of a city Orbi" arise "simply from a desire to get rid of what seemed a strange miracle, at the cost of substituting for it a gross improbability." For how, he asks, could regular sustenance by human hands of a hunted prophet have gone on "in the face of the jealous vigilance of the king"? To explain away one wonder in a life "teeming with miracles" is idle.

Ellicott then lifts the verse into its larger place. This period, he observes, is "the second great epoch of recorded miracle in the Old Testament," the struggle of Elijah and Elisha against the Baal apostasy answering to "the still more critical epoch of Moses and Joshua" that was the first; and he warns that to pronounce this or that miracle "so improbable as to introduce any difficulty of acceptance" is a difficulty that "does not apply to miracles in general." The three commentators do not really contend among themselves; against the whole tendency to soften the marvel, they stand together. The birds of prey that scripture elsewhere counts unclean and rapacious are made God's faithful caterers, and the lesson is the sovereignty of a God who can command the least likely creatures to keep his prophet alive.
5:
Only one witness speaks at this verse, and he speaks briefly, yet the words themselves repay the pause. "So he went, and did according to the word of the Lord": the Reformed John Gill unfolds it in a single breath — Elijah "took his journey eastward, and hid himself in the place directed to." The whole of the prophet's greatness, in this sentence, is compressed into an act of exact obedience. He is told in v.3 to get hence, turn eastward, and hide by Cherith; here, without a word of his own recorded, he does precisely that.

The phrase Gill lingers over is the chapter's quiet refrain. "According to the word of the Lord" governs the prophet at the brook here, and it will govern the widow at Zarephath, who likewise "did according to the saying of Elijah" (v.15), and it will seal the standing miracle of the meal and oil, which failed not "according to the word of the Lord" (v.16). The narrator is teaching by repetition: the same obedience that hides the prophet feeds him, and the same word that appoints the ravens (v.4) is honoured by the man who trusts it.

There is something bracing in what Gill's terse note leaves unsaid. Elijah does not argue that a brook is a poor refuge, or that ravens are unclean and unlikely servants, or that hiding is beneath a prophet who has just faced down a king. The command carries no explanation with it, and he asks for none. To go where God sends, to drink of the appointed brook, and to wait upon unlikely providence is the whole of his task, and he performs it at once. The verse thus stands as the hinge between the promise of v.4 and its fulfilment in v.6: God has spoken, the prophet has obeyed, and the ravens may now be trusted to come. Where later the widow's faith will be tried and found great, here the prophet's faith is shown in the plainest possible form — a man who simply went and did as he was told, and so was kept alive.
6:
The promise of v.4 is now fulfilled to the letter — bread and flesh, morning and evening — and the two witnesses attend to the wonder of its regularity. The Reformed John Gill notes that these were "the two principal meals then in use," provided ready-dressed, "the bread made and baked, and the flesh boiled, broiled, or roasted." He weighs the old question of where the ravens fetched it: the Jews say from Ahab's table, others from Jehoshaphat's, and others, "as probable as any," from the tables of the seven thousand who had not bowed the knee to Baal (1 Kings 19:18). Gill's instinct is to let the source stay hidden; the marvel is that it came at all, and came twice daily without fail.

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke pauses first on the text. The Vatican manuscript of the Septuagint and some ancient fathers read that the ravens brought "bread in the morning, and flesh in the evening," but Clarke observes that "all the other versions agree with the Hebrew text," which gives both bread and flesh at each meal. Then he draws a homelier inference than Gill's: this is "the first account we have of flesh-meat breakfasts and flesh-meat suppers," and since it was the fare God appointed for his prophet, "we may naturally conjecture that it was the food of the people at large."

The two readings are complementary rather than opposed. Gill looks up from the food to its unseen source and finds the hand of providence; Clarke looks down at the menu and finds a window on ancient diet. Yet both are held by the same astonishment that runs through the whole scene. Birds that scripture reckons unclean, that neglect even their own young, are made punctual servants, carrying not carrion but wholesome man's meat to a single man by a drying stream. The regularity is the point the narrative presses: not one great deliverance but a steady, unglamorous provision, morning by morning and evening by evening, teaching the prophet — as the ravens taught him — to receive each day's bread in the day, and to leave tomorrow's supply, like the source of today's, in the keeping of God.
7:
The idyll by the brook does not last, and the two witnesses read its ending as both a note of time and a note of doctrine. The Reformed John Gill parses the Hebrew phrase rendered "after a while" as literally "at the end of days," and suggests it may mark the passing of a year, that being a frequent sense of the idiom. The stay at Cherith was no brief interlude but a long, hidden season, ended only when the water gave out.

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke supplies the reason the brook failed: "because there had been no rain in the land for some time, God having sent this drought as a testimony against the idolatry of the people," and he points back to the sanction Moses had pronounced, that apostasy would shut up the heavens (Deuteronomy 11:16). The drying of Cherith is thus no accident but the drought doing its appointed work, reaching at last even the prophet's own refuge.

Here the narrative turns a searching corner, and the two comments together sharpen it. Elijah's food had come by miracle, but his drink came "in an ordinary way from the brook," and it is the ordinary provision that fails first. The very judgment the prophet had announced now touches his own comfort: the same withheld rain that punishes rebellious Israel dries the stream that had kept him alive. Yet the failure is God's, not the ravens'; the birds are still faithful, and it is the natural supply, not the miraculous, that runs dry. The lesson the witnesses leave implicit is one the chapter will make explicit at Zarephath — that creature-comforts are lent, not owned, and are apt to fail us in the very hour we lean on them, so that we may learn to draw from a fountain that never runs dry. There is instruction, too, in the order of the failures: the brook that the ravens' Lord had appointed serves faithfully until the drought's slow work overtakes it, so that even here God's provision does not so much break down as reach its set term. For the present the prophet is left with an empty brook and a fresh silence, waiting once more for the word of the Lord to tell him where to turn (v.8), and learning by the loss of his stream the same trust the widow will learn by the emptying of her jar.
8:
With the brook dry, the recurring formula returns — "the word of the Lord came unto him" — and the witnesses read the new command as a fresh unfolding of grace. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch mark the timing (Gill reckons it after a year at Cherith) and the destination: Zarephath, "which belongeth to Zidon," where God says, "I have commanded a widow woman there to sustain thee." The Reformed John Gill feels the sharpness of the sending — it was "a trial of the prophet's faith, to be sent to dwell in a place belonging to the Zidonians, among whom Jezebel had an interest, being the daughter of their king."

The Puritan Matthew Henry makes this verse his great meditation, and he presses two marvels. The first is the variety of God's care: "When the brook was dried up Jordan was not; why did not God send him thither?" Because "he has a variety of ways to provide for his people and is not tied to any one." The second is the destination's daring. Zarephath is a Gentile city, and our Saviour himself marked it as an early token of favour to the nations, that when "many widows were in Israel in the days of Elias," the prophet was sent to none of them but to a woman of Sidon (Luke 4:26) — so that Elijah becomes, in Dr. Lightfoot's phrase, "the first prophet of the Gentiles." Henry sees a pointed providence in the choice: Baal-worship had come into Israel from Sidon with Jezebel (1 Kings 16:31), and out of Sidon God will fetch the reformer who confronts it. Driven out by his own countrymen, Elijah "turns to the Gentiles, as the apostles were afterwards ordered to do" (Acts 18:6).

Henry lingers, too, on the chosen hostess. Not a rich merchant of Sidon nor a governor like Obadiah, but "a poor widow woman, destitute and desolate, is commanded — that is, is made both able and willing — to sustain him." It is God's way and his glory "to make use of the weak and foolish things of the world and put honour upon them," for he is "in a special manner the widows' God." And there is a bitter irony that becomes a comfort: Jezebel was Elijah's deadliest enemy, "yet, to show her the impotency of her malice, God will find a hiding-place for him even in her own country." The witnesses do not divide; they gather round one wonder — that the God who had shut up Israel's heaven opens a poor widow's door in a heathen town, and makes her want the very means of the prophet's keeping.
9:
The name of the town draws the witnesses into geography, and they converge with unusual ease. The Reformed John Gill, the Wesleyan Adam Clarke, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott agree in the essentials: this is the Sarepta of the Septuagint and of the New Testament (Luke 4:26), a place Josephus set between Tyre and Sidon and Jerome located on the great coast-road, commonly identified with the modern village Surafend, or Sarphan. Gill and Clarke both cite the traveller Maundrell, who found only a few houses on the hilltops but supposed the ancient city had stood below on the plain, "where there are still ruins of a considerable extent." Gill is careful, too, to distinguish this Zarephath from another of the same name, and to weigh whether it belonged to the land of Israel or, as is likelier, was a Gentile city.

Where the reading gains its edge is in the added clause, "which belongeth to Zidon." Charles Ellicott takes these words to be emphatic, "marking the striking providence of God, which, when the land of Israel was apostate and unsafe, found for the prophet a refuge and a welcome in a heathen country, which was moreover the native place of his deadliest enemy." The very soil that bred Jezebel is made to shelter the man she would kill.

The verse, small as it is, thus carries the paradox the chapter keeps pressing. Gill had already felt it as a trial of Elijah's faith to be lodged in Sidonian country; Ellicott turns the same fact into a display of divine irony and tenderness. There is no dispute among the three — only a widening of the one thought — and it lands where the narrative means it to: that God's provision is not bound by the borders of the covenant people. A prophet exiled from idolatrous Israel is bidden to "arise, get thee to Zarephath, and dwell there," and the place appointed is neither grand nor safe by human reckoning, but a poor heathen town under the shadow of his enemy's household, where a widow he has never met has already, by God's secret command, been made ready to receive him.
10:
Again a single verse and a single, brief witness, yet the sentence is a whole act of faith. "So he arose, and went to Zarephath": the Reformed John Gill adds only the measured distance, that the place "according to Bunting was one hundred miles from the brook Cherith." Behind that bare figure lies the greatness of the prophet's obedience. Told in v.9 to arise and go, he arises and goes, and the journey itself is no small thing.

Gill's one detail is worth dwelling on, for it measures what obedience cost. A hundred miles is a long way on foot in the best of times; in a famine it is a long way through a parched and failing land, from a dried brook east of Jordan to a Gentile town on the far Phoenician coast, much of the road passing through the very kingdom whose king and queen were hunting the prophet's life. Elijah does not object that the errand is dangerous, or that a hundred miles is a great distance to travel on the word that an unnamed widow in a foreign city will feed him. He simply "arose, and went."

The verse gathers up the pattern the chapter has been drawing. At Cherith he "went, and did according to the word of the Lord" (v.5); here the same prompt compliance carries him a hundred miles to a stranger's door. The provision he is walking toward is as improbable as the ravens were — a destitute widow, herself down to a last handful of meal, is to be his host — and yet he goes on nothing but the promise. The narrator lets the plainness stand: no vision on the road, no assurance renewed along the way, only a command received and a command performed. When the prophet reaches the city gate, he will find the woman gathering sticks for what she thinks will be her last meal; but that meeting belongs to the next verse. Here there is only the walking, and the quiet lesson that faith's first work is to set out. The God who had commanded the ravens has now, unseen, commanded a widow (v.9), and the prophet's part is simply to travel toward a provision already prepared for him a hundred miles away.
11:
The prophet reaches the gate and makes his first request, and the two witnesses read the exchange as a testing on both sides — the woman's kindness tested, and her identity confirmed. The Reformed John Gill dwells on the readiness of her response: when Elijah asks for water, "she made no difficulty of granting his request, but immediately set out to fetch him some," being "very ready to do an hospitable act to a stranger, and especially to a good man, and a prophet, as she might perceive by his habit he was." Then, as she goes, he calls after her for something more — "Bring me, I pray thee, a morsel of bread in thine hand."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain the escalation. Elijah asks first for water and then for bread "in order to determine with indisputable certainty whether this believing Gentile was the protectress assigned him by the Lord." The water she grants without a word; the bread draws out her true condition. She answers with an oath by Jehovah that she has nothing baked — only "a handful of meal in the כּד," the meal-pail, "and a little oil in a cruse" — and that she is at that moment "gathering wood to dress this remnant for herself and her son," that they may eat it and die.

Read together, the two comments frame the poignancy of the meeting. Gill sees a poor woman whose first movement, even in famine, is generous; Keil and Delitzsch see a prophet using the smallest of requests as a divining instrument, watching to see whether God's promise of v.9 is being kept in this very person. The two readings meet in the widow's answer. Her willingness to fetch water shows the open heart God had prepared; her confession of a last handful of meal shows how little she has to give, and so how much the giving will cost. The stage is exactly set for the hard word Elijah is about to speak — "make me thereof a little cake first" (v.13) — a demand that will turn this chance-looking encounter at a city gate into the trial and triumph of a Gentile widow's faith.
12:
The widow's reply — "As the Lord thy God liveth" — is the hinge of her portrait, and the witnesses weigh both her piety and her poverty. The Reformed John Gill takes the oath as a good sign: it "shows her to be a good woman, swearing by the living God, and him only, and that she took Elijah to be a good man, and a prophet of the Lord." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the same words more cautiously. The phrase indeed recognises Elijah "as a prophet of Jehovah the God of Israel," but the pronoun in "thy God" seems to imply "no acknowledgment of Him as yet by the woman herself" — the courteous recognition a neighbouring heathen might yield, as Hiram once did in the days of Solomon, without personal faith. It is a distinction the chapter will resolve only at its close, when she confesses the word in his mouth to be truth (v.24).

On her poverty the witnesses agree, and Ellicott sets it in its wider history: the famine "may have already extended to Phoenicia," where, according to Menander, it lasted a year, or the distress may have been only "the reflex effect of the famine in Israel," on which the coast lands depended for supplies. Either way she is at the end of her store. The Wesleyan Adam Clarke attends to the homely vessels named — the "barrel" is the Hebrew כּד, "an earthen jar, not a wooden vessel," in which the people of the East kept their meal safe from insects, and the "cruse" is a bottle, as the French rabbi Jarchi glossed it.

The three readings compose a single, moving figure. Here is a woman devout enough to swear by the living God and to honour his prophet, yet standing at the very edge of starvation, with a handful of meal in her jar and a little oil in her bottle, gathering two sticks to bake a last meal for herself and her son before they die. Whether her faith in Israel's God is as yet her own or only her neighbours' — the point on which Gill and Ellicott gently differ — it is real enough to be tried, and the trial is at hand. Into that extremity Elijah will speak his astonishing "Fear not" (v.13), asking the destitute to give first and trust for the rest.
13:
Elijah's answer to a starving woman is at once a comfort and an astonishing demand, and the two witnesses feel the force of both. The Reformed John Gill hears the tenderness first: "Fear not" means, do not fear "that she and her son should die, it would not be the case." Before any command is laid on her, the dread that fills her last sentence in v.12 — "that we may eat it, and die" — is met and answered. The prophet does not begin with a burden but with a promise of life.

Yet the command that follows is staggering, and the Wesleyan Adam Clarke does not soften it: "This was certainly putting the widow's faith to an extraordinary trial: to take and give to a stranger, of whom she knew nothing, the small pittance requisite to keep her child from perishing, was too much to be expected." The order of the words is the whole trial — "make me thereof a little cake first, and bring it unto me, and after make for thee and for thy son." She is asked to feed the prophet before she feeds her own child, out of a supply she believes will not stretch to one more meal.

Between Gill's "Fear not" and Clarke's "extraordinary trial" the verse holds its tension without resolving it, and that is the point. The comfort is real, but it is offered in the form of a test; the promise of life is genuine, but it must be seized by an act that looks, to sight, like throwing away the last of life's means. Everything turns on the little word "first." To give God his portion out of a little, before providing for oneself, and to do it on the strength of a bare word from a stranger, is the very shape of faith the chapter means to hold up. Elijah asks nothing he does not immediately back with the promise of v.14, that the meal and oil shall not fail; but for the moment the widow stands with the sticks in her hand and the hardest of choices before her, bidden to fear not and to give first.
14:
The demand of the previous verse is now underwritten by a promise, and the witnesses mark how the word of the God of Israel steadies a Gentile heart. The Reformed John Gill notes the significance of the very name Elijah invokes — "Thus saith the Lord God of Israel" — a God "whom the prophet perceived she had knowledge of, and faith in." The pledge is precise: "The barrel of meal shall not waste, neither shall the cruse of oil fail, until the day that the Lord sendeth rain upon the earth." The supply is guaranteed not by a lump sum but by a daily renewing, and it is bound to the drought's own end, so that her provision and the nation's judgment run out together.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the promise straight into its keeping. She obeyed, and "she, and he, and her house, did eat many days"; the barrel of meal did not waste nor the cruse of oil fail, "according to the word of the Lord, which he spake by Elijah." The stress falls on that closing clause: the miracle is the visible honouring of a spoken word, God making good in a poor kitchen exactly what his prophet had said in his name.

The two comments, brief as they are, hold the theological weight of the whole episode. Gill fixes on the promise as the object of the woman's faith — she believed the word of a God not her own by birth — while Keil and Delitzsch fix on the fulfilment as the vindication of that word. What links them is the shape of the assurance itself. The meal and oil are not multiplied into a store she can count and hoard; they are renewed only as they are used, so that every day she must go again to the jar in fresh dependence, and every day find enough and no more. It is a promise perfectly fitted to teach trust: not abundance that would tempt her to forget God, but a daily sufficiency that keeps her looking to him, until the rain returns and the ordinary mercies of harvest resume. The pledge given here will be tested in the doing (v.15) and sealed in the outcome (v.16).
15:
The promise is met by obedience, and the witnesses read the widow's act and its wonder in complementary keys. The Reformed John Gill fixes on the order of her doing: "she went, and did according to the saying of Elijah," which he understands to mean she "made a cake for him first, and brought it to him" — and this, he says, "showed great faith in the word of the Lord by him." The whole trial of v.13 turned on that word "first," and Gill notes that she passed it, giving the prophet his portion before providing for herself and her son.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott turns from the woman's faith to the miracle that rewarded it, and finds it "doubly remarkable." First, "as in the similar miracles of Elisha and of our Lord Himself, we see that God's higher laws of miracle, like the ordinary laws of His providence, admit within their scope the supply of what we should consider as homely and trivial needs" — a point that runs against what we might have expected, that miracle should be reserved for grander things. Second, this is "a miracle of multiplication, which is virtual creation — not necessarily out of nothing — doing rapidly and directly what, under ordinary laws, has to be done slowly and by indirect process." The same power that fills the fields with grain over months fills the widow's jar in a moment.

The two readings answer to the two halves of the verse. Gill looks at "she went, and did," and sees faith; Ellicott looks at the "many days" of supply that followed, and sees the character of the divine working. Together they hold the episode's balance. The miracle does not dispense with the woman's obedience but crowns it: she must first bake and bring the cake, staking her last meal on a stranger's promise, and only then does the meal prove inexhaustible. Nor is the wonder any less wonderful for being domestic. That God should stoop to keep a poor widow's flour-jar from emptying is of a piece with the God who numbers hairs and feeds sparrows, and it prepares the household — and the reader — for the greater sign still to come, when the same prophet's God will give back a dead child alive.
16:
The standing miracle is now stated in its own words — "the barrel of meal wasted not, neither did the cruse of oil fail" — and both witnesses reach at once for the Gospel parallels that light it up. The Reformed John Gill explains the mechanism as no mechanism at all but a "continual increase and supply of both, through the mighty power of God working a continued miracle," and he likens it to the feeding of the multitude, where "the loaves and fishes were increased while the disciples were eating" (John 6:11). The wonder is not a single act but a sustained one, renewed with every meal for two years and more.

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke pictures the daily experience of it: "She continued to take out of her jar and out of her bottle the quantity of meal and oil requisite for the consumption of her household; and without carefully estimating what was left, she went with confidence each time for a supply, and was never disappointed." He too finds a Gospel likeness, comparing it to the miracle at Cana, where "as the servants drew the water out of the pots, they found it turned into wine," and continued to draw until the guests were fully supplied (John 2:9).

The two comments are of one mind, and their shared instinct is telling: to read this Old Testament wonder forward into the works of Christ, so that the widow's inexhaustible jar becomes a small anticipation of the Bread of Life who multiplies and never runs out. Both fasten, too, on the same spiritual note in Clarke's description — that the increase came "in the spending," not in the hoarding. She never took stock, never measured the dwindling remnant, but went each day in simple confidence and found enough. The miracle thus preaches the very lesson the whole chapter has been teaching by the ravens and the brook: that God's people are kept not by a visible store laid up against the future, but by a daily sufficiency drawn in fresh dependence, "and was never disappointed." What was promised in v.14 is here fulfilled to the letter, and the household that had faced death by famine is carried through it by a jar that would not empty.
17:
The chapter's brightest scene is broken by its darkest turn, and the two witnesses read the child's sickness and death as a fresh trial sent into the very house of blessing. The Reformed John Gill sets the timing: this came to pass "not only after the conversation that passed between the prophet and the widow, but after they had lived together many days, a year or years, upon the miraculous provision made for them." Into that settled mercy the blow falls.

The Puritan Matthew Henry makes the affliction his theme, and he presses first the mystery of its timing. The boy "was fed miraculously, and yet that did not secure him from sickness and death"; as our Lord said, "Your fathers did eat manna, and are dead, but there is bread of which a man may eat and not die" (John 6:49). Henry reads the loss as "a thorn in the flesh, lest she should be lifted up above measure with the favours that were done her and the honours that were put upon her." Two things sharpen it. She was "nurse to a great prophet," had every reason to expect only good, "yet now she loses her child"; and she was herself "nursed by miracle," kept without cost by a distinguishing blessing, "and in the midst of all this satisfaction she was thus afflicted."

From these Henry draws his two great cautions. "We must not think it strange if we meet with very sharp afflictions, even when we are in the way of duty, and of eminent service to God." And, "When we have the clearest manifestations of God's favour and good-will towards us, even then we must prepare for the rebukes of Providence. Our mountain never stands so strong but it may be moved; and therefore, in this world, we must always rejoice with trembling." The witnesses do not divide; Gill supplies the settled prosperity into which the sorrow comes, and Henry draws out its meaning, and both hold the same hard truth — that neither faith nor obedience, neither the prophet's presence nor the miracle of the meal, shuts affliction and death out of the door. The blow will find its voice in the mother's bitter cry (v.18); but already the lesson is set, that the surest tokens of God's love are no guarantee against his sharpest rebukes, and that those most favoured must walk most humbly.
18:
The mother's cry — "What have I to do with thee, O thou man of God? art thou come unto me to call my sin to remembrance, and to slay my son?" — is read by the witnesses as the anguished speech of a newly awakened conscience. The Reformed John Gill hears it as spoken "rashly, and in her passion and agony," a grief so extreme that it made her "forget and overlook all the benefits she had received." The Wesleyan Adam Clarke locates its root in guilt: "she seems to be now conscious of some secret sin, which she had either forgotten, or too carelessly passed over," and, he adds, "it is mostly in times of adversity that we duly consider our moral state; outward afflictions often bring deep searchings of heart."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott traces a growth even in her distress. Her title for Elijah — "man of God" — is now the "regular Israelitish description of the prophet as her own" (compare Judges 13:6), a step beyond the mere "thy God" of v.12; the fuller knowledge gained by the prophet's presence has bred in her "a deeper sense of sin." Behind her words Ellicott hears the cry of Peter, "Depart from me, for I am a sinful man, O Lord" (Luke 5:8), and he catches the "pathetic confusion of idea" by which she cries out against Elijah's presence "as if it were the actual cause of judgment on the sin, which it has simply brought home to her conscience."

The one real question the witnesses raise is why the child was taken, and here Keil and Delitzsch part company with the woman's own reading. She, and Clarke with her, take the death as a punishment recalling her sin; but the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch answer that it was, "like the blindness in the case of the man born blind" (John 9:3), "not sent as a punishment for particular sins, but intended as a medium for the manifestation of the works of God in her." They find in her half-heathenish complaint "a mind susceptible to divine truth and conscious of its sin, to which the Lord could not refuse His aid." So the witnesses turn even this rash and bitter outburst toward hope: the grief that makes her accuse the prophet is the very sense of sin that God is using to draw her, and the death she reads as ruin is the appointed occasion of the greatest sign she will ever see.
19:
The prophet answers the mother's bitter accusation not with a word of defence but with an action and a prayer, and the two witnesses dwell on the tenderness and the boldness of it. The Reformed John Gill notes the gentleness: "Give me thy son," and "he took him out of her bosom" — the place where she had laid him, mourning over him — "with her leave." Elijah does not argue with her grief; he takes the dead child into his own care, carrying him up to the loft where he lodged and laying him on his own bed.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch follow him into that chamber and weigh the cry he raises there: "Jehovah, my God! hast Thou also brought evil upon the widow with whom I sojourn, to slay her son?" The little word "also," they observe, "refers to the other calamities occasioned by the drought." Yet they are careful about its tone: the words "contain no reproach of God, but are expressive of the heartiest compassion for the suffering of his benefactress and the deepest lamentation, which, springing from living faith, pours out the whole heart before God in the hour of distress." The meaning they draw out is a wrestling confidence: "Thou, O Lord my God, according to Thy grace and righteousness, canst not possibly leave the son of this widow in death." And they add the ground of the prophet's boldness — "such confident belief carries within itself the certainty of being heard."

The two comments together sketch the movement of a great intercessor. Gill shows the compassion that stoops to lift the child from the weeping mother's arms; Keil and Delitzsch show the faith that carries the case straight to God and refuses to believe he will leave it there. The verse turns the mother's despairing question inside out: she had asked whether Elijah came to slay her son, and Elijah takes her son and lays him before the God of life. What in her mouth was an accusation becomes in his mouth a plea, and the same providence she blamed is the one he now importunes. His action anticipates his prayer; he does not wait to be told the child can be raised, but proceeds at once, as men do who are sure of the God they address, to the wrestling of v.21.
20:
Elijah's cry over the dead child — "O Lord my God, hast thou also brought evil upon the widow with whom I sojourn, by slaying her son?" — is weighed by the witnesses for its very tone, and here they gently differ. The Reformed John Gill notes only the manner of it: he "cried unto the Lord, or prayed unto him, as the Targum, with great vehemence and importunity." The intensity is agreed on all sides; its temper is not.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott hears in the complaint "the half-presumptuous impatience seen more fully" in the later Elijah of 1 Kings 19. He takes the prophet to imply "that his own lot, as a hunted fugitive not protected by God's Almighty power, is so hard, that it must be his presence which has brought trouble even on the home that sheltered him." On this reading the cry carries a note of self-concern, even of expostulation. Keil and Delitzsch, treating the same words at the previous verse, read them oppositely — as containing "no reproach of God," but the pure lamentation of a faith that pours out its whole heart. The one hears a trace of impatience; the other, only importunate trust.

Keil and Delitzsch then turn to the strange act that accompanies the prayer, the prophet stretching himself three times upon the child, and they are at pains to say what it was not. It was not, as with Paul over Eutychus (Acts 20:10), to ascertain whether life remained, "for the youth there was only apparently dead"; nor, as with Elisha over the Shunammite's son (2 Kings 4:34), to warm the body and stir its blood, "for the action of Elisha is described in a different manner"; but "to bring down the vivifying power of God upon the dead body, and thereby support his own word and prayer." Sebastian Schmidt is cited to the point: "This was done, that the prophet's body might be the instrument of the miracle, just as in other cases of miracle there was an imposition of the hand." Whether Elijah's cry be flecked with impatience or wholly composed, the witnesses agree on where its power lies: not in the prophet's body or his passion, but in the God he importunes, who alone can make the dead alive.
21:
The prophet stretches himself three times upon the child and prays, "Let this child's soul come into him again," and the witnesses divide over what the gesture meant. The Reformed John Gill reads it as an act of intense affection and identification: Elijah "measured himself" on the boy, "putting his mouth on its mouth, his eyes on its eyes, his hands on its hands," as Elisha would afterward do (2 Kings 4:34), thereby stirring himself to more fervent and importunate prayer, and signifying "that he would, if he could, infuse his breath and life into it."

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke presses a more physical explanation: "It is supposed that he did this in order to communicate some natural warmth to the body of the child, in order to dispose it to receive the departed spirit," as Elisha and, perhaps, Paul over Eutychus (Acts 20:10) did the like. But the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott will have none of this reduction: "To suppose that this implies merely the use of some natural means of reviving the dead, is simply to explain the whole description away." The true idea, he holds, is "of a certain healing virtue, attaching in measure to the person of the prophets, as without measure it belonged to our Lord Himself" (Luke 8:45) — yet, crucially, "made distinctly conditional on prayer," since "the Lord heard the voice of Elijah."

So the one real debate here is whether the stretching was a natural means or a channel of divine power — Clarke's warmth to dispose the body against Ellicott's healing virtue that is no mere physic. Beneath it runs a second interest that Clarke himself opens: the words "let his soul come into him." Though the Hebrew נֶפֶשׁ may sometimes mean the breath or the life, Clarke finds "the expressions here singular" — the soul returning "into the midst of him" — and takes them as evidence of an immortal, immaterial spirit that can exist apart from the body. The witnesses do not settle every point, but they close ranks on the decisive one: whatever the prophet's outward act, the child lives only because God, entreated, sends the soul back; the virtue is real, but it is virtue on its knees.
22:
The prayer is answered, and the witnesses gather round the plain, staggering fact: "the Lord heard the voice of Elijah; and the soul of the child came into him again, and he revived." The Reformed John Gill states it with fitting economy — the Lord "heard the voice of Elijah in prayer, and answered it." The whole weight of the miracle rests on that hearing; the prophet's stretching and crying issue in nothing until God acts, and then the dead child breathes.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch lift the raising into its redemptive place. Through this wonder, they write, "Elijah showed himself as the forerunner of Him who raiseth all the dead to life," and by it "the pious Gentile woman was mightily strengthened in her faith in the God of Israel." What the meal and oil had begun, the restored child completes: she moves from receiving a prophet to trusting his God, a movement the next verses will voice.

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke fastens instead on the language of the soul's return — "the soul of the child came into him again," literally "into the midst of him." He argues that the Hebrew נֶפֶשׁ here "generally means the immortal spirit," and that the phrase furnishes "a strong proof, not only of the existence of an immortal and immaterial spirit in man, but also that that spirit can and does exist in a separate state from the body." It is "here represented as being in the midst of the child, like a spring in the center of a machine, which gives motion to every part, and without which the whole would stand still." The three witnesses press three facets of one event — Gill the answered prayer, Keil and Delitzsch the sign of resurrection, Clarke the immortal soul — and they leave no daylight between them on the substance. The God who feeds by ravens and fills an empty jar is the God who calls a departed soul back into a cold body, and the prophet who announced the drought is revealed as the forerunner of the Lord of life.
23:
The miracle is completed in a homely gesture — the prophet carrying the living child down the stairs to his mother — and the two witnesses linger on its tenderness. The Reformed John Gill traces the simple movements: the boy "being come to life, he took it off of the bed in his bedchamber, and brought it down to the lower part of the house, where the woman was." Elijah does not summon her up to the wonder; he brings the wonder down to her, and puts into her arms the son she had given up for dead, with the quiet words, "See, thy son liveth."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the scene to its effect on the mother's faith. Through this restoration "the pious Gentile woman was mightily strengthened in her faith in the God of Israel"; she now "not only recognised Elijah as a man of God, as in v.18, but perceived that the word of the Lord in his mouth was truth," and so "confessed implicitly her faith in the God of Israel as the true God." The delivering-back is not the end of the story but the ground of her confession in v.24.

The two comments hold together the human and the theological. Gill gives us the picture — a prophet descending the narrow stair with a breathing boy, a mother's arms and disbelief — and Keil and Delitzsch give us its fruit, a Gentile heart brought home to Israel's God. It is worth noticing how the scene foreshadows a greater one: when the Lord of life met a widow of Nain at her only son's bier, he raised the young man and "delivered him to his mother" (Luke 7:15), in the very posture of Elijah here. The prophet who was sent to a foreign widow, and fed by her, now repays her beyond all reckoning, and the God who had seemed to slay her son is shown to be the God who gives him back. The word "See" is almost the whole of it — grief turned to sight, and sight, in the next verse, to confession.
24:
The chapter ends in a confession — "Now by this I know that thou art a man of God, and that the word of the Lord in thy mouth is truth" — and the witnesses read it as the summit of a faith built by stages. The Reformed John Gill traces the ascent: she took Elijah for a man of God at his first coming, "was in a great measure confirmed in it by the miracle of the barrel of meal and cruse of oil," was staggered when her son died, "but now, by his resurrection from the dead, was fully assured of it." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott marks the same steps precisely: first she had spoken of Jehovah from without, as "the Lord thy God" (v.14); next she came to recognise him as God (v.18); now, "in accepting the word of Jehovah in his mouth as the truth," she "seems undoubtedly to express conversion to Him," a growth Ellicott likens to the stages of faith in the nobleman at Capernaum (John 4:53).

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke gathers the fruit of the whole ordeal — "through a little suffering all this good was obtained" — naming three effects: the woman was led to examine her heart, the power of God was manifested in the resurrection, and she was convinced "that not one syllable of God's word could fall to the ground." But Clarke then reopens, at length, the old question of v.4: who really fed Elijah at the brook? He marshals every difficulty against literal ravens — the bird is unclean by Leviticus 11:15, feeds on carrion, and its use would require a whole tissue of miracles where God works "by the simplest means" — and, canvassing Bochart, proposes instead that עֹרְבִים means merchants, or Arabians, or best of all the inhabitants of a town Orbo near Beth-shan, citing the rabbis and Jerome, and noting that Jerome elsewhere renders the same word "Arabians" (Nehemiah 4:7).

Here at last the witnesses genuinely divide. Against Clarke's ingenious townsmen stands the plain reading that Ellicott had defended at v.4 — "ravens," with almost all the ancient versions and Josephus — and Gill too had simply taken the birds at their word. The dispute is real but bounded, and it does not touch the confession that crowns the chapter. Whether ravens or men brought the bread, and however the meal was multiplied and the child raised, the widow's verdict is the narrator's own: the man is God's, and God's word does not lie. A Gentile mother, tried by famine and bereavement, ends where the whole story has been driving — at faith in the God of Israel and the truth of his word.
